segments.text: W- welcome back everyone to the Planning Bill and Natural
Environment Bill, hearings this afternoon.
I can see that we already have our next submitter with us,
Airbnb. Uh, so we've got Catherine,
we can see you there, and Louise. Uh, welcome
to the committee this afternoon. You have 20 minutes with
us, so, look, we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your
submission and make some opening remarks, and then we'll open it up for
questions. So over to you.
Wonderful. Thank you for the opportunity to address the committee
today. Uh, my name's Catherine Cossevas, and I'm the acting head of
public policy at Airbnb for Australia and New
Zealand. I am joined today by Louise Espin, senior
associate at Russell McVeagh. I will start by spending the
first eight to 10 minutes sharing information about Airbnb and key
points from our submission. Uh, my comments relate specifically to
short-term accommodation and the opportunity presented by the
planning bill to introduce a nationally consistent regulatory framework
for the sector. Short-term accommodation is a vital
component of the New Zealand economy, supporting local jobs and
small businesses throughout the country.
Our community of hosts are everyday Kiwis who are passionate about
hosting and showcasing all that their communities have to
offer. They provide a range of accommodation options
at a variety of price points. Short-term accommodation also
plays an important role in the, in the visitor economy.
Every day, guests use our platform to travel within New Zealand,
whether it be for a holiday, a work trip, to visit friends and
family, or to attend a major event.
We strongly support the government's broader objectives for planning reform,
particularly in simplifying the planning system, improving
consistency, and enabling economic growth.
And our key message today is, is quite simple.
As New Zealand undertakes comprehensive planning reform, it would be a
missed opportunity not to address greater national
standardization and enablement of short-term accommodation
regulation at the same time. In terms of our overall
position, short-term accommodation is currently regulated in a
highly fragmented way across New Zealand.
Different councils use different definitions of short-term
accommodation. There are different consent requirements, different
operating thresholds, and, and different zoning rules.
And some examples of this include different definitions.
So some plans only define one activity, for example, in
Queenstown, that would be residential visitor accommodation, whereas
others have two separate definitions, as in Christchurch,
where both hosted visitor accommodation and unhosted visitor
accommodation is defined. And another example is differing
rules by activity status. Short-term accommodation is a permitted
activity with permitted standards in most, but not all
residential type zones in key cities and towns.
And with 78 councils that maintain separate district plans with
varying rules, zones, and consent pathways for short-term
accommodation, hosts and platforms like Airbnb
must navigate a patchwork of rules, and this often creates regulatory
uncertainty, unnecessary compliance costs,
administrative burdens for councils and other organizations,
as well as individuals that otherwise need to actively get involved in
long and expensive plan review, or plan change
processes, and then potentially appeals also.
Um, and so this can result in inconsistent outcomes, for
communities. And, and to that end, naturals-- national
standardization would be entirely consistent with one of the central
goals of planning reform itself, which is greater national
consistency. Short-term accommodation is, is fundamentally a
land use issue. It concerns how residential
property is used. The government has emphasized enabling
people to use their private property as they wish, provided it does
not create adverse effects for neighbors.
The basic activities taking place in, in short-term accommodation properties
are sleeping, eating, bathing. Uh, this is the same as, as
any residential use. In many jurisdictions internationally,
including parts of Australia and the United States,
short-term accommodation is treated as a form of residential use, for
this reason. There is therefore no clear justification for
treating short-term accommodation fundamentally differently from
other residential occupancy.And as you're
aware, the planning bill already introduces nat- national
instruments, including national standards, which would be the natural
place to address this issue. National standards would allow
short-term accommodation to be treated uniformly across the country,
significantly reducing regional discrepancies, and
our key submission is, is in support of the planning bill, which sets up the
framework for national standards to be established for national
consistency, for issues such as this.
And so while the content of national standards will be developed at subsequent
stages, Airbnb's submission focuses on the
processes for developing the standards being set up in the right
way, including requiring key stakeholder engagements in the preparation of
national instruments and allowing more than 20 days for
submissions. We, we'd actually suggest 40 working
days. And if, if short-term accommodation is not dealt with on a
nationally consistent basis under this planning reform process, it would then
require resource intensive processes under each land use plan
and result in further costs for councils, stakeholders, and also
individuals. And so for efficiency and clarity, it makes sense
to address short-term accommodation properly now, once,
and within this reform process. Um, but I'll now touch on the
economic and tourism benefits of, of short-term rental
accommodation. Um, so the benefits are closely aligned with the planning
bill's objective of supporting and enabling economic
growth, and change through the development and use of
land. Short-term rentals contribute significantly to New Zealand's
visitor economy, providing accommodation choice, flexible
supply, options across different locations and different
price points. Importantly, short-term accommodation spreads
tourism spending beyond traditional accommodation hubs,
including in smaller and remote areas of New Zealand that do not have
many or any, hotel or motels.
In 2025, our host community welcomed over 3.3 million
guests, to New Zealand. In 2024,
Airbnb guests who stayed across the country spent an estimated
$4.4 billion on accommodation and non-accommodation
expenses, supporting more than 33,000 jobs,
according to research by Oxford Economics.
And guests in short-term accommodation spend money in local cafes, in
restaurants, shops, transport services and, and visitor
attractions as well. And this, of course, directs tourism dollars into
local communities, that may not have otherwise benefit from, from
tourism. Hosting also supports Kiwi
households. In a recent survey of Airbnb hosts in New
Zealand, almost 40% said the income from hosting
helps them to stay in their home, and this shows that short-term accommodation
provides meaningful support for many households.
For, for so many Kiwis, hosting isn't merely a side activity, but
it's a practical way to manage rising living costs and service mortgage
repayments, particularly for first home buyers and also owner
occupiers. And given these benefits, it, it is important that
overly restrictive or inconsistent regulations don't
unnecessarily limit responsible hosting.
Doing so could undermine a valuable source of income for Kiwi households and
also reduce the flow of tourism revenue that supports local businesses
and local communities.
Um, and as I mentioned, the current fragmented system creates significant
administrative burdens for local councils who are currently
interpreting, enforcing, and updating complex and differing
short-term accommodation rules.
The Queenstown District Plan review litigation, for example,
lasted over six years and is a clear example of how fragmented
regulation can lead to lengthy disputes, costly legal
proceedings, uncertainty for, for all
stakeholders, and substantial use of council
resources. A clear and national framework that
focuses on better enabling short-term accommodation and reducing the re-
requirements for resource consents for certain activities
would reduce the need for litigation, streamline compliance, and allow
councils to focus their resources on more pressing planning priorities
such as enabling housing supply. Um, and so in
closing, we strongly support the objectives of the planning
bill. Establishing clear and consistent national rules for short-term
rentals would directly support those objectives by
reducing regulatory fragmentation, as I mentioned, improving
efficiency in the planning system, supporting economic
growth, and enabling responsible use of residential
property. Um, and as I've mentioned, this reform process
presents a unique opportunity to address New Zealand's approach to short-term
accommodation properly. Um, and we would welcome the opportunity to work
constructively with government on the development and drafting of the national
standards. Our goal is to work together to help get the
framework right the first time. Uh, but thank you very much,
for your time.
Look, thank you very much, Catherine, for that, submission.
It's, interesting to hear from short-term
rentals and accommodation, 'cause we haven't yet.
